Understanding the Children of Immigrant Families

Updated April 5, 2024 | Posted October 21, 2021
By the Annie E. Casey Foundation

In recent years, chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies regôÙuôÙlarôÙly have been at the cenôÙter of pubôÙlic polôÙiôÙcy disôÙcusôÙsions, such as those regardôÙing the southôÙern borôÙder of the UnitôÙed States and the Deferred Action for ChildôÙhood Arrivals polôÙiôÙcy, among othôÙers. To proôÙmote an accuôÙrate underôÙstandôÙing of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion and conôÙstrucôÙtive conôÙverôÙsaôÙtions about how to supôÙport these chilôÙdren and their famôÙiôÙlies, it is imporôÙtant to underôÙstand basic terôÙmiôÙnolôÙoôÙgy and key facts about this group in the UnitôÙed States.

DefôÙiôÙnôÙiôÙtions About ChilôÙdren in ImmiôÙgrant Families

ImmiôÙgrant or Foreign-born

The terms immiôÙgrant and forôÙeign-born are interôÙchangeôÙable and refer to indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals who were not U.S. citôÙiôÙzens at birth but may have become citôÙiôÙzens through natôÙuôÙralôÙizaôÙtion. Those who are not citôÙiôÙzens may include lawôÙful perôÙmaôÙnent resôÙiôÙdents, those with temôÙpoôÙrary visas, refugees and asyôÙlum seekôÙers or undocôÙuôÙmentôÙed immiôÙgrants.

U.S. CitôÙiôÙzens

CitôÙiôÙzenôÙship usuôÙalôÙly is acquired when a child is born in the UnitôÙed States, PuerôÙto Rico, Guam, the U.S. VirôÙgin Islands or the NorthôÙern MarôÙiôÙanas or born abroad to AmerôÙiôÙcan parôÙents. CitôÙiôÙzenôÙship also can be obtained through the natôÙuôÙralôÙizaôÙtion process.

NatôÙuôÙralôÙizaôÙtion

This is the process in which a lawôÙful perôÙmaôÙnent resôÙiôÙdent becomes a U.S. citôÙiôÙzen after meetôÙing requireôÙments in the ImmiôÙgraôÙtion and NationôÙalôÙiôÙty Act.

Kids under age 18 can become natôÙuôÙralôÙized citôÙiôÙzens if at least one parôÙent natôÙuôÙralôÙizes or if they are adoptôÙed by a U.S. citôÙiôÙzen parôÙent. Adults genôÙerôÙalôÙly can qualôÙiôÙfy for natôÙuôÙralôÙizaôÙtion after being a perôÙmaôÙnent resôÙiôÙdent for at least five years, or three years if marôÙried to a U.S. citôÙiôÙzen, along with meetôÙing othôÙer speôÙcifôÙic criôÙteôÙria. IndiôÙvidôÙuôÙals can also qualôÙiôÙfy based on milôÙiôÙtary serôÙvice. Many parôÙents are eliôÙgiôÙble to natôÙuôÙralôÙize and may need supôÙport to take the steps to become a U.S. citôÙiôÙzen, which could help their famôÙiôÙlies access imporôÙtant pubôÙlic serôÙvices, legal rights, new job opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties and othôÙer benefits.

LawôÙful PerôÙmaôÙnent ResôÙiôÙdents (also known as ããgreen cardã holders)

This perôÙmaôÙnent staôÙtus may be obtained through mulôÙtiôÙple methôÙods, includôÙing famôÙiôÙly relaôÙtionôÙships, employôÙment sponôÙsorôÙships, humanôÙiôÙtarôÙiôÙan proôÙtecôÙtion (for refugees and asyôÙlum seekôÙers) or the DiverôÙsiôÙty Visa ProôÙgram lottery.

AsyôÙlum SeekôÙers and Refugees

These are indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals who need proôÙtecôÙtion due to perôÙseôÙcuôÙtion or fear of perôÙseôÙcuôÙtion ããon account of race, reliôÙgion, nationôÙalôÙiôÙty and/ãor memôÙberôÙship in a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar social group or politôÙiôÙcal opinôÙion.ã AsyôÙlum seekôÙers subôÙmit appliôÙcaôÙtions at a port of entry to the UnitôÙed States or when they are already in the counôÙtry, while refugees are usuôÙalôÙly outôÙside of the UnitôÙed States when they are conôÙsidôÙered for resettlement. 

UndocôÙuôÙmentôÙed Immigrants

This term refers to forôÙeign-born indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals who are not citôÙiôÙzens and not legal resôÙiôÙdents. This includes peoôÙple who entered the UnitôÙed States withôÙout inspecôÙtion and those who were legalôÙly admitôÙted on a temôÙpoôÙrary basis but stayed beyond their required deparôÙture date.

Migrants

GenôÙerôÙalôÙly, ããmigrantsã refers to peoôÙple who changed their counôÙtry of usuôÙal resôÙiôÙdence regardôÙless of the reaôÙson. It may be volôÙunôÙtary or forced migraôÙtion, and it may be temôÙpoôÙrary or permanent.

NativôÙiôÙty

NativôÙiôÙty refers to whether indiôÙvidôÙuôÙals are native-born verôÙsus forôÙeign-born. Native-born is defined as born in the UnitôÙed States, PuerôÙto Rico, Guam, the U.S. VirôÙgin Islands or the NorthôÙern MarôÙiôÙanas or born abroad to AmerôÙiôÙcan parents. 

First-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion ImmiôÙgrant Children

This term typôÙiôÙcalôÙly refers to forôÙeign-born chilôÙdren with at least one forôÙeign-born parent. 

SecôÙond-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion ImmiôÙgrant Children

This term typôÙiôÙcalôÙly refers to native-born chilôÙdren with at least one forôÙeign-born parent. 

ChilôÙdren in ImmiôÙgrant Families

GenôÙerôÙalôÙly, this term includes both first- and secôÙond-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion immiôÙgrant children.

How Many ChilôÙdren Are in ImmiôÙgrant FamôÙiôÙlies in the UnitôÙed States? 

One in 4 chilôÙdren (25%) ã or 18 milôÙlion kids ã were in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies in 2022, up from 1 in 5 (19%) in the earôÙly 2000s. The share of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies also increased in all but three states, and it at least douôÙbled in 16 states, over this time period.


While this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion has grown conôÙsidôÙerôÙably durôÙing the last 20 or more years, births to forôÙeign-born mothôÙers have not mirôÙrored this increase. In 2021 (latôÙest data availôÙable on the KIDS COUNTôÛ Data CenôÙter), 21% of births were to forôÙeign-born women, the same share as in 2000. This perôÙcentôÙage rose to 25% in 2006 but has since dropped back down to its preôÙviôÙous level.

How many youth and young adults are immiôÙgrants or live in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies in the UnitôÙed States?

About 10.5 milôÙlion, or 22%, of young peoôÙple ages 14 to 24 were forôÙeign-born or lived with at least one forôÙeign-born parôÙent in 2017ã2021. This popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion has grown from 9.6 milôÙlion, or 20%, in 2006ã2010.

What states have the largest share of chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrant families?

SevôÙen states conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly have ranked at the top in recent years, with the biggest shares in the counôÙtry: CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia (44% in 2022), New JerôÙsey (41%), New York (36%), FloriôÙda (34%), MassôÙaôÙchuôÙsetts and NevaôÙda (both 33%) and Texas (32%). Half of the nationãs 18 milôÙlion chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies reside in just four of these states: CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia (3.7 milôÙlion in 2022), Texas (2.4 milôÙlion), FloriôÙda (1.5 milôÙlion) and New York (1.4 million).

View all state-levôÙôÙel data on chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant families

What states have the largest share of these youth and young adults?

SimôÙiôÙlar to the patôÙterns for kids, states with the highôÙest perôÙcentôÙages of oldôÙer youth ages 14 to 24 who are immiôÙgrants or live with at least one immiôÙgrant parôÙent include CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia (44% in 2017ã2021), NevaôÙda and New JerôÙsey (both 34%), New York (32%), FloriôÙda (31%) and Texas (30%). These six states have had the highôÙest shares of youth and young adults in immiôÙgrant houseôÙholds for all years availôÙable on the KIDS COUNT Data Center. 

More than half (53%) of these 10.5 milôÙlion young peoôÙple live in the same four states notôÙed for chilôÙdren above: CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia, Texas, New York and Florida. 

See data for all states

What parts of the counôÙtry have seen the greatôÙest increase in shares of chilôÙdren and youth of immiôÙgrant families?

OverôÙall, five states expeôÙriôÙenced the biggest rise in shares of chilôÙdren, youth and young adults in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies durôÙing the past 15 to 20 years: MaryôÙland, MassôÙaôÙchuôÙsetts, New JerôÙsey, VirôÙginia and WashôÙingôÙton. SpeôÙcifôÙic findôÙings by age group and state are proôÙvidôÙed below.

ChilôÙdren under age 18 

Over the last two decades, the folôÙlowôÙing states and D.C. ã all but one locatôÙed in the EastôÙern region of the UnitôÙed States ã expeôÙriôÙenced the largest increase in chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies, with jumps of 10 to 17 perôÙcentôÙage points:

  • MaryôÙland: from 15% in 2000ã2002 to 32% in 2022
  • New JerôÙsey: 27% to 41%
  • MassôÙaôÙchuôÙsetts: 20% to 33%
  • WashôÙingôÙton: 18% to 30%
  • Delaware: 9% to 21%
  • DisôÙtrict of ColumôÙbia: 17% to 28%
  • VirôÙginia: 13% to 24%
  • GeorôÙgia: 10% to 21%
  • North CarôÙoliôÙna: 9% to 19%

Young peoôÙple ages 14 to 24

DurôÙing the 15-year span in which data are availôÙable for youth and young adults in immiôÙgrant houseôÙholds, the folôÙlowôÙing states saw the most growth in shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion, with upticks of 4 to 5 perôÙcentôÙage points:

  • MassôÙaôÙchuôÙsetts: from 20% in 2006ã2010 to 25% in 2017ã2021
  • MaryôÙland: 19% to 24%
  • New JerôÙsey: 30% to 34%
  • WashôÙingôÙton: 20% to 24%
  • VirôÙginia: 15% to 19%
  • MinôÙnesoôÙta: 12% to 16%
  • PennôÙsylôÙvaôÙnia: 8% to 12%
  • VerôÙmont: 5% to 9%

How do states vary by chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrantsã parental regions of origin?

As nationôÙal conôÙtext, the majorôÙiôÙty of U.S. chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies have parôÙents origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from Latin AmerôÙiôÙca, although this share has declined from 62% in 2005 to 57% in 2022. The share with parôÙents from Asia, on the othôÙer hand, has increased from 22% to 25% in the same time periôÙod. The perôÙcentôÙage with parôÙents from Africa rose as well, from 4% to 8%. ChilôÙdren of immiôÙgrants with parôÙents from Europe declined slightôÙly, from 10% to 9%, durôÙing this timeframe.

ParôÙent region of oriôÙgin: Latin America

At the state levôÙel in 2022, chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with parôÙents origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from Latin AmerôÙiôÙca were most likeôÙly to live in the folôÙlowôÙing westôÙern and southôÙern states, among those with availôÙable data:

  • New MexôÙiôÙco: 81% 
  • FloriôÙda: 78%
  • AriôÙzona: 72% 
  • Arkansas: 72%
  • Texas: 71%
  • OklaôÙhoma: 70%

HowôÙevôÙer, these states are not where most of the growth occurred in the past couôÙple decades. Texas has actuôÙalôÙly expeôÙriôÙenced the biggest decrease in shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of any state, with a drop of 10 perôÙcentôÙage points between 2005 and 2022, and AriôÙzona had a drop of 9 perôÙcentôÙage points in this periôÙod. New MexôÙiôÙco and Arkansas also saw small declines of 4 and 2 points, respecôÙtiveôÙly. The greatôÙest growth in shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion occurred in five othôÙer states since the mid-2000s: 

  • TenôÙnessee: from 44% in 2005 to 56% in 2022
  • ConôÙnectiôÙcut: 40% to 52%
  • WisôÙconôÙsin: 41% to 52%
  • South CarôÙoliôÙna: 51% to 61%
  • PennôÙsylôÙvaôÙnia: 31% to 41%

ParôÙent region of oriôÙgin: Asia 

Hawaii and MichiôÙgan by far have the largest shares of kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with parôÙents origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from Asia, at 64% and 54% in 2022, respecôÙtiveôÙly. The next highôÙest shares were in WashôÙingôÙton and VirôÙginia in 2022, with 35%.

While Hawaii and MichiôÙgan conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly have the greatôÙest shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion, these states are expeôÙriôÙencôÙing oppoôÙsite trends over time. Between 2005 and 2022, Hawaii had the secôÙond-largest decline in this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion nationôÙwide, with a drop of 9 perôÙcentôÙage points (Louisiana had the biggest drop in the counôÙtry, from 37% to 22%). MichiôÙgan, conôÙverseôÙly, saw the most growth in shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of any state in the last 17 years, with a 9 perôÙcentôÙage point jump between 2005 and 2022. Five states folôÙlowed Michiganãs upward traôÙjecôÙtoôÙry, with increasôÙes of 6 to 8 perôÙcentôÙage points: IdaôÙho and AriôÙzona (both rose from 9% to 17%), CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia (25% to 32%), OreôÙgon (21% to 27%) and Texas (12% to 18%).

ParôÙent region of oriôÙgin: Africa

MinôÙnesoôÙta, Ohio and MaryôÙland conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly have the greatôÙest perôÙcentôÙages of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with parôÙents origôÙiôÙnatôÙing from Africa, among states with availôÙable data: 35%, 27% and 25% in 2022, respecôÙtiveôÙly. These three states have been home to the largest shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion since 2015.

Two of these states ã MinôÙnesoôÙta and Ohio ã also expeôÙriôÙenced the most growth in shares of this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion since the mid 2000s, folôÙlowed by KenôÙtucky and Iowa:

  • MinôÙnesoôÙta: from 17% in 2005 to 35% in 2022
  • Ohio: 15% to 27%
  • KenôÙtucky: 8% to 19%
  • Iowa: 6% to 17%

ParôÙent region of oriôÙgin: Europe

In 2022, the greatôÙest shares of kids of immiôÙgrants with parôÙents from origôÙiôÙnatôÙing Europe were in ConôÙnectiôÙcut (19%), IlliôÙnois (16%) and South CarôÙoliôÙna (16%). While state figôÙures on this meaôÙsure vary from year to year, ConôÙnectiôÙcut conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly has among the highôÙest perôÙcentôÙages in the country. 

Although this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion has not grown as a perôÙcentôÙage nationôÙwide over the last 17 years, it has shiftôÙed withôÙin the counôÙtry, with Rhode Island, KenôÙtucky, PennôÙsylôÙvaôÙnia and ConôÙnectiôÙcut each seeôÙing decreasôÙes of at least 10 perôÙcentôÙage points. No sinôÙgle state saw a large jump in this timeôÙframe, but the biggest increase occurred in Hawaii, with a 3ãpercentage point bump from 4% in 2005 to 7% in 2022.

How many chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies are U.S. citizens? 

In 2022, 89% of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies were U.S. citôÙiôÙzens, an increase from 84% in 2005. This is conôÙsisôÙtent with the CenôÙsus Bureauãs findôÙing that the vast majorôÙiôÙty (nearôÙly 90%) of chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrants are born in the UnitôÙed States.

How many U.S. chilôÙdren, overôÙall, are foreign-born?

Among all chilôÙdren (not just those in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies), about 2.6 milôÙlion, or 4%, were born in anothôÙer counôÙtry, accordôÙing to 2022 data. This figôÙure has hovôÙered at 3% or 4% since the KIDS COUNT Data CenôÙter began trackôÙing it in 2005. These kids may be U.S. citôÙiôÙzens by natôÙuôÙralôÙizaôÙtion or not citôÙiôÙzens. As notôÙed, chilôÙdren who are not U.S. citôÙiôÙzens may lawôÙful perôÙmaôÙnent resôÙiôÙdents, those with visas, refugees and asyôÙlum seekôÙers or undocôÙuôÙmentôÙed immigrants.


How many chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies do not have parôÙents who are U.S. citôÙiôÙzens (i.e., neiôÙther parôÙent at home is a citizen)? 

An estiôÙmatôÙed 35% of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies did not have resôÙiôÙdent parôÙents who were U.S. citôÙiôÙzens in 2022 ã a figôÙure that has declined from 42% over the past. 

How many chilôÙdren of immiôÙgrants have parôÙents who recentôÙly came to the UnitôÙed States?

NationôÙwide, only 3% of kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies (about 600,000 in 2022) live with parôÙents who have been in the counôÙtry five years or less. In othôÙer words, nearôÙly all chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies do not have resôÙiôÙdent parôÙents who are new to the UnitôÙed States. This figôÙure has remained steady at 3% or 4% since 2005. At the state levôÙel in 2022, this figôÙure ranged from just 1% in NevaôÙda up to 6% in Ohio and 7% in MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi, among states with data.

Among young peoôÙple, what share of each racial and ethôÙnic group are first- or secôÙond-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion immigrants? 

NationôÙalôÙly, about 75% of Asian and PacifôÙic Islander youth and young adults ages 14 to 24 were immiôÙgrants or lived in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies in 2017ã2021. The same was true for 50% of LatiôÙnos and 32% among those of two or more races, folôÙlowed by 17% of AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan or AlasôÙka Native, 15% of Black and 6% of white youth. 


Among states with data in 2017ã2021, the share of young peoôÙple in each racial and ethôÙnic group who were first- or secôÙond-genôÙerôÙaôÙtion immiôÙgrants varôÙied wideôÙly:

  • Asian and PacifôÙic Islander youth and young adults: New JerôÙsey had the highôÙest share of immiôÙgrants or those in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies, at 84%, while Hawaii had the lowôÙest, at 47%.
  • LatiôÙno youth and young adults: State shares ranged from a high of 64% in MaryôÙland to a low of 14% in Montana.
  • Two or more races: MonôÙtana had the smallôÙest perôÙcentôÙage (4%) of these youth ages 14 to 24 in immiôÙgrant houseôÙholds, while FloriôÙda (47%) had the largest.
  • AmerôÙiôÙcan IndiôÙan or AlasôÙka Native youth and young adults: In New York, nearôÙly half (47%) of these young peoôÙple were immiôÙgrants or in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies, while the share in Wyoming was less than 1%.
  • Black youth and young adults: MinôÙnesoôÙta had the highôÙest share in the counôÙtry, at 54% in immiôÙgrant houseôÙholds, while Arkansas and MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi had the lowôÙest, at 1%.
  • White youth and young adults: State figôÙures ranged from highs of 15% in CalôÙiôÙforôÙnia and New JerôÙsey to lows of 1% in Arkansas, MisôÙsisôÙsipôÙpi and West Virginia.

Income and EmployôÙment StaôÙtisôÙtics for ChilôÙdren and Youth in ImmiôÙgrant Families

EcoôÙnomôÙic staôÙbilôÙiôÙty proôÙvides a critôÙiôÙcal founôÙdaôÙtion for healthy child develôÙopôÙment. When parôÙents are unemôÙployed or earn low wages, their abilôÙiôÙty to meet basic needs and access resources to supôÙport their kids is limôÙitôÙed, which can underôÙmine their childrenãs well-being and prospects in school and beyond. EconomôÙic hardôÙship can also disôÙrupt childrenãs cogôÙniôÙtive develôÙopôÙment and physôÙiôÙcal and menôÙtal health. These impacts reverôÙberôÙate across the lifesôÙpan. ChilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies genôÙerôÙalôÙly have disôÙparate access to resources and face greater hurôÙdles to ecoôÙnomôÙic staôÙbilôÙiôÙty comôÙpared to chilôÙdren in non-immiôÙgrant families.

How many youth in immiôÙgrant houseôÙholds live in high-poverôÙôÙôÙty areas? 

One in 10 (10%) of these young peoôÙple ages 14 to 24 lived in high-poverôÙôÙty areas in 2018ã2022, down from 20% in 2009ã2013. ããHigh-poverôÙty areasã refer to cenôÙsus tracts with poverôÙty rates of 30% or more. At the state levôÙel, this figôÙure was as low as 2% in New HampôÙshire and as high as 22% in New MexôÙiôÙco in 2018ã2022.

What share of young peoôÙple in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies live in low-income households?

About 2 in 5 (39%) youth and young adults who were immiôÙgrants or resided with immiôÙgrant parôÙents lived in low-income houseôÙholds in 2018ã2022. Among states with availôÙable data, this figôÙure ranged from a low of 25% in New HampôÙshire up to 55% in Mississippie. 

How likeôÙly are chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies to live in low-income workôÙing households? 

In 2022: 29% of kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies were livôÙing in low-income workôÙing houseôÙholds comôÙpared to 20% of kids in U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies. The difôÙferôÙence between these two groups has narôÙrowed over the last decade. While the share of chilôÙdren in low-income workôÙing famôÙiôÙlies increased by two perôÙcentôÙage points for both groups from 2021 to 2022, the figôÙure for chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies is lowôÙer now than it was durôÙing the preôÙviôÙous decade, when it stayed above 30%.


How many chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies have parôÙents who lack secure employment?

About 4.2 milôÙlion, or nearôÙly one in four, kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies live in houseôÙholds where no parôÙent has regôÙuôÙlar, full-time employôÙment, accordôÙing to 2022 data. This figôÙure steadiôÙly fell over the past decade from 28% in 2012 to 21% in 2019, and then jumped to 27% in 2021 before dropôÙping back down to 23% in 2022. Among states with data in 2022, this staôÙtisôÙtic varôÙied wideôÙly, from less than one in ten (8%) kids in South DakoôÙta to nearôÙly one in three (30%) in Louisiana.

What is the mediôÙan income for immiôÙgrant and non-immiôÙôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with kids?

The mediôÙan income for immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with chilôÙdren was $81,900 in 2022, comôÙpared to $90,400 for U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies with chilôÙdren. HouseôÙholds with chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly have had lowôÙer mediôÙan incomes in recent decades, although incomes subôÙstanôÙtialôÙly increased for both houseôÙhold types since 2012, and the gap has narrowed.

HousôÙing StaôÙtisôÙtics for ChilôÙdren in ImmiôÙgrant Families 

Safe, staôÙble and affordôÙable housôÙing is critôÙiôÙcal for child health and well-being. StaôÙble housôÙing roots chilôÙdren, young peoôÙple and famôÙiôÙlies in comôÙmuôÙniôÙties, enabling them to access opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties, build social netôÙworks and have conôÙsisôÙtent eduôÙcaôÙtion and neighôÙborôÙhood expeôÙriôÙences, all of which proôÙvide an imporôÙtant founôÙdaôÙtion to thrive. HousôÙing staôÙbilôÙiôÙty is linked to many posôÙiôÙtive outôÙcomes for chilôÙdren and famôÙiôÙlies, includôÙing betôÙter eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal achieveôÙment, employôÙment staôÙbilôÙiôÙty and physôÙiôÙcal and menôÙtal health. 

UnforôÙtuôÙnateôÙly, kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies are more likeôÙly to expeôÙriôÙence housôÙing instaôÙbilôÙiôÙty than their peers in non-immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies. ObtainôÙing affordôÙable housôÙing is a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar chalôÙlenge. When famôÙiôÙlies have to pay too much for rent or a mortôÙgage, it limôÙits their capacôÙiôÙty to afford othôÙer necesôÙsiôÙties, such as food, health care, transôÙportaôÙtion and child care. UnafôÙfordôÙable housôÙing can also lead to othôÙer risks such as foreôÙcloôÙsures and evicôÙtions, famôÙiôÙlies movôÙing in with othôÙers (crowdôÙed housôÙing), movôÙing to less safe conôÙdiôÙtions or homelessness.

How does the abilôÙiôÙty to afford housôÙing comôÙpare between kids born to immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies and kids in U.S.-born families? 

In 2022, 37% of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies were livôÙing in houseôÙholds with a high housôÙing cost burôÙden (i.e., more than 30% of monthôÙly income spent on housôÙing) verôÙsus 28% of chilôÙdren in U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies. While housôÙing cost burôÙdens have declined for both groups since 2012, this disôÙparôÙiôÙty has perôÙsistôÙed since 2000ã2002, the earôÙliôÙest years of data availability. 


How does home ownôÙerôÙship comôÙpare for immiôÙgrant and non-immiôÙôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies with children?

In 2022, chilôÙdren in U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies were more likeôÙly to live in houseôÙholds that owned their homes (68%) comôÙpared to chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies (58%). This home ownôÙerôÙship disôÙparôÙiôÙty between immiôÙgrant and non-immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies has perôÙsistôÙed for two decades. 

Why does home ownôÙerôÙship matter?

Experts argue that increasôÙing affordôÙable home ownôÙerôÙship, in addiôÙtion to affordôÙable rental housôÙing, is a critôÙiôÙcal way to improve housôÙing staôÙbilôÙiôÙty and affordôÙabilôÙiôÙty for vulôÙnerôÙaôÙble famôÙiôÙlies. Major advanôÙtages to home ownôÙerôÙship include staôÙble or declinôÙing housôÙing costs over time (with a fixed-rate mortôÙgage) comôÙpared to rental costs that typôÙiôÙcalôÙly increase over time, as well as the abilôÙiôÙty to build wealth through home equiôÙty. A home ownerãs mortôÙgage payôÙment each month can be seen as an investôÙment in an asset, whereôÙas each rent payôÙment is an expense. An analyôÙsis by the Urban InstiôÙtute also found that low-income home ownôÙers were actuôÙalôÙly less burôÙdened by housôÙing costs than low-income renters. RecôÙogôÙnizôÙing the chalôÙlenges that immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies face in obtainôÙing staôÙble housôÙing, leadôÙers can purôÙsue strateôÙgies to expand affordôÙable home ownôÙerôÙship for this popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion and low-income famôÙiôÙlies in general.

What share of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies live in crowdôÙed housôÙing comôÙpared to chilôÙdren in non-immiôÙôÙgrant families?

ChilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies are close to three times more likeôÙly to live in crowdôÙed housôÙing (defined as more than one perôÙson per room, includôÙing livôÙing rooms, dinôÙing rooms, etc.). In 2022, 26% of kids in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies lived in crowdôÙed housôÙing, an improveôÙment from 36% in 2000ã2002. In these same years, howôÙevôÙer, just 9% or 10% of chilôÙdren in U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies were livôÙing in crowdôÙed housôÙing. OverôÙcrowdôÙing, specifôÙiôÙcalôÙly, is linked to negôÙaôÙtive social, eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal, physôÙiôÙcal and menôÙtal health outôÙcomes for children.

EduôÙcaôÙtion and LanôÙguage StaôÙtisôÙtics for ChilôÙdren in ImmiôÙgrant Families 

ProôÙmotôÙing eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal achieveôÙment requires ongoôÙing qualôÙiôÙty eduôÙcaôÙtion, learnôÙing enviôÙronôÙments and supôÙport from earôÙly childôÙhood through young adultôÙhood. Many chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies face greater hurôÙdles to eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal sucôÙcess than chilôÙdren in non-immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies. The eduôÙcaôÙtion and lanôÙguage indiôÙcaôÙtors proôÙvidôÙed below ã as well as our countryãs perôÙsisôÙtent acaôÙdôÙeôÙmôÙic disôÙparôÙiôÙties by race, income and EngôÙlish proôÙfiôÙcienôÙcy ã demonôÙstrate the need for addiôÙtionôÙal efforts to proôÙvide equiôÙtable opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties for sucôÙcess. LeadôÙers can priôÙorôÙiôÙtize the needs of these chilôÙdren, supôÙport their strengths like bilinôÙgualôÙism, and reduce barôÙriôÙers to achievement. 

How likeôÙly are chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies to have no parôÙent at home with a high school diploôÙma or equivalent?

NearôÙly one in five (18%) chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies had no parôÙent with a high school diploôÙma in 2022, a decrease from 29% in 2000ã2002. This same staôÙtisôÙtic is much lowôÙer among chilôÙdren in U.S.-born famôÙiôÙlies and declined from 9% to 5% over the same two decades.

What perôÙcentôÙage of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies live in linôÙguisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly isoôÙlatôÙed households? 

In 2022, 16% of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies lived in linôÙguisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly isoôÙlatôÙed houseôÙholds, a figôÙure that has dropped by 12 perôÙcentôÙage points since reachôÙing 28% in the earôÙly 2000s. States with the greatôÙest shares in 2022 were Louisiana (26%), TenôÙnessee (24%) and KenôÙtucky (21%), among those with availôÙable data. (Note: A linôÙguisôÙtiôÙcalôÙly isoôÙlatôÙed houseôÙhold is one in which no perôÙson age 14 or oldôÙer speaks only EngôÙlish, and no perôÙson age 14 or oldôÙer who speaks EngôÙlish as a secôÙond lanôÙguage speaks it very well.) 

How likeôÙly are fourth-grade EngôÙlish lanôÙguage learnôÙers to score below proôÙfiôÙcient in reading? 

NationôÙwide, 90% of fourth graders who are EngôÙlish lanôÙguage learnôÙers scored below proôÙfiôÙcient in readôÙing in 2022. By comôÙparôÙiôÙson: 64% of their EngôÙlish-fluôÙent classôÙmates did not achieve readôÙing proôÙfiôÙcienôÙcy that year.

U.S. Child PopôÙuôÙlaôÙtion Growth 

The overôÙall U.S. child popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion has decreased in size in recent decades, accordôÙing to a recent report comôÙmisôÙsioned by the Annie E. Casey FounôÙdaôÙtion. At the same time, the popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion size of chilôÙdren in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies has increased, as notôÙed above, conôÙtributôÙing to the growôÙing diverôÙsiôÙty of Americaãs chilôÙdren. A declinôÙing U.S. child popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion means a future decrease in the workôÙforce which, in turn, places greater imporôÙtance on kidsã health and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal outôÙcomes so that they can become viable workôÙers. The shrinkôÙing child popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion not only eleôÙvates the imporôÙtance of each child to our countryãs future prosôÙperôÙiôÙty, it also eleôÙvates the role of immiôÙgrants as a source of popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion and ecoôÙnomôÙic growth.

For all chilôÙdren to thrive, the basic needs of every young perôÙson ã from every demoôÙgraphôÙic group ã must be met. The increasôÙing racial, ethôÙnic and linôÙguisôÙtic diverôÙsiôÙty of U.S. chilôÙdren means that leadôÙers must invest in culôÙturôÙalôÙly responôÙsive strateôÙgies to supôÙport the well-being of young peoôÙple. The FounôÙdaôÙtion urges polôÙiôÙcyôÙmakôÙers and othôÙer leadôÙers to priôÙorôÙiôÙtize poliôÙcies and proôÙgrams that would expand opporôÙtuôÙniôÙties for Americaãs chilôÙdren, youth and young adults in immiôÙgrant famôÙiôÙlies, along with othôÙer vulôÙnerôÙaôÙble groups that conôÙsisôÙtentôÙly face barôÙriôÙers to success.

Learn More About ChilôÙdren of Immigrants

The Annie E. Casey FounôÙdaôÙtion offers a range of reports and resources to proôÙmote the health and well-being of chilôÙdren and famôÙiôÙlies, includôÙing the immiôÙgrant popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion. Explore the resources below and sign up for our newsletôÙters to conôÙtinôÙue learning. 

  • See all data by famôÙiôÙly nativity on the KIDS COUNT Data Center
  • Report: Race for Results: BuildôÙing a PathôÙway to OpporôÙtuôÙniôÙty for All ChilôÙdren (2024)
  • Report: RoadôÙblocks to WorkôÙforce IncluôÙsion for Young Adult ImmiôÙgrants (2022)
  • Resource Roundup: SupôÙportôÙing EngôÙlish LanôÙguage LearnôÙers (2022)

AddiôÙtionôÙal resources from the MigraôÙtion PolôÙiôÙcy InstiôÙtute:

  • Report: A Path to MeetôÙing the MedôÙical and MenôÙtal Health Needs of UnacôÙcomôÙpaôÙnied ChilôÙdren in U.S. ComôÙmuôÙniôÙties (2023)
  • Report: Four StrateôÙgies to Improve ComôÙmuôÙniôÙty SerôÙvices for UnacôÙcomôÙpaôÙnied ChilôÙdren in the UnitôÙed States (2022)

  • Immigration and Refugees
  • Racial Equity and Inclusion
